title: Israel’s general election will be like no other before
gist: Israel's 27 October 2026 election is projected to end in another stalemate: opposition parties poll ahead of Netanyahu's coalition but refuse both to govern with Arab parties and with Netanyahu, likely leaving them short of the 61 seats needed and forcing repeat elections. The prolonged interim period, during which a weakened Netanyahu retains full power to launch military action, is flagged as a period of acute escalation risk across Gaza, Lebanon, Iran and the West Bank.

insights.claim: Israel's general election is set for 27 October 2026, and current polling projects another prolonged political stalemate rather than a decisive outcome.
insights.speaker: Chatham House
insights.so_what: A hung result means a lengthy caretaker-government period and elevated policy/geopolitical uncertainty for regional risk positioning.
insights.quote: As matters stand, the projected results point to another prolonged political stalemate.
insights.locator: As matters stand, the projected results

insights.claim: Opposition parties are polling better than the current governing coalition, but most Zionist parties refuse to ally with Arab parties, leaving the opposition short of the 61 seats needed for a majority in the 120-seat Knesset.
insights.speaker: Chatham House
insights.so_what: The refusal to build an Arab-inclusive bloc is the structural reason a poll lead cannot convert into governing power, sustaining deadlock.
insights.quote: leaving the opposition short of the 61 seats required for a majority in the 120-member Knesset
insights.locator: But most Zionist parties still insist

insights.claim: Opposition parties have pledged not to form a government with Benjamin Netanyahu, a stance rooted first in his status as a defendant in three corruption cases and now reinforced by his refusal to take responsibility for 7 October 2023.
insights.speaker: Chatham House
insights.so_what: With both a Netanyahu government and an Arab-inclusive government ruled out, the mathematically viable coalition paths are narrow, raising the odds of repeat elections.
insights.locator: Moreover, the opposition parties have so far maintained

insights.claim: Unless the vote produces a far more decisive result than polling suggests or a camp abandons its campaign pledges, Israel is likely to head back to the polls again after 27 October in search of a governing majority.
insights.speaker: Chatham House
insights.so_what: Base case is one or more repeat election rounds, extending the caretaker window and its associated instability.
insights.locator: Unless the election produces a far more decisive result

insights.claim: Netanyahu's 2022 coalition, formed to keep him in power amid his corruption trial, made the most extreme right-wing, ultranationalist and messianic elements in Israeli politics indispensable partners who pushed judicial reforms to weaken checks and balances.
insights.speaker: Chatham House
insights.so_what: The coalition's dependence on radical partners is framed as the root driver of Israel's democratic backsliding and instability, a durable structural risk beyond any single election.
insights.locator: This election is taking place in extraordinary circumstances

insights.claim: The Hamas cross-border attack of 7 October 2023 killed nearly 1,200 people and took 251 hostages, after which Israel became embroiled in simultaneous conflicts with Gaza, Lebanon, Syria, Yemen and Iran with no clear end on any front.
insights.speaker: Chatham House
insights.so_what: A multi-front, open-ended war posture keeps Israel's fiscal, security and diplomatic exposure elevated regardless of who wins.
insights.quote: nearly 1,200 people, many of them civilians, were killed, and 251 taken hostage
insights.locator: At this critical moment, Hamas launched

insights.claim: Netanyahu's bet that President Trump would prosecute the war with Iran until the Tehran regime was destroyed has backfired, with Iran remaining intact and US-Israel relations at a low ebb.
insights.speaker: Chatham House
insights.so_what: A frayed US-Israel relationship removes a key backstop and increases the chance of unilateral Israeli escalation; watch for further daylight between Washington and Jerusalem.
insights.quote: Netanyahu’s gamble that President Donald Trump would prosecute the war with Iran until the regime in Tehran was destroyed has backfired: Iran remains intact.

insights.claim: Israel is defending itself against genocide allegations at the International Court of Justice while the International Criminal Court has issued arrest warrants for Netanyahu, leaving the country more internationally isolated than at any point in recent decades.
insights.speaker: Chatham House
insights.so_what: Legal and diplomatic isolation constrains trade, travel and defense ties for Israel and raises reputational risk for counterparties; a durable overhang.
insights.locator: The government is defending itself against allegations of genocide

insights.claim: For the first time, Iranian missiles have struck Israeli towns and cities killing civilians, and Hezbollah attacks have forced the evacuation of tens of thousands of residents from northern Israel.
insights.speaker: Chatham House
insights.so_what: A breached homeland-security perimeter signals a structurally higher domestic risk premium for Israeli assets and northern-region economic activity.
insights.quote: Iranian missiles have struck Israeli towns and cities for the first time, killing civilians.

insights.claim: At least ten parties are likely to clear the 3.25 per cent electoral threshold, producing a highly fragmented Knesset that makes forming and sustaining a government harder.
insights.speaker: Chatham House
insights.so_what: Extreme fragmentation raises coalition-building costs and the probability of unstable or short-lived governments.
insights.quote: At least ten parties appear likely to surpass the 3.25 per cent electoral threshold
insights.locator: At least ten parties appear likely

insights.claim: Legislation to entrench ultra-Orthodox exemptions from compulsory military service cuts across traditional political divisions and may prove decisive for voters who served in the military or did reserve duty since October 2023.
insights.speaker: Chatham House
insights.so_what: The conscription-exemption fight is a swing issue that could reshuffle coalition math beyond the usual left-right lines.

insights.claim: The final weeks of the current Knesset have seen a rush of controversial legislation aimed at expanding executive power at the expense of judicial independence and media freedom.
insights.speaker: Chatham House
insights.so_what: Last-minute institutional changes could outlast the coalition and shift Israel's governance risk profile even if the government falls.
insights.quote: a rush of controversial legislation, much of it aimed at expanding executive power at the expense of judicial independence and media freedom
insights.locator: The final weeks of the current Knesset

insights.claim: Despite Netanyahu's unprecedented failures, the principal opposition parties fail to offer a clear policy alternative, mostly promising to pursue similar objectives with greater competence rather than a different strategic direction.
insights.speaker: Chatham House
insights.so_what: A change of government would likely not mean a change of strategy, so regional-policy continuity should be the base case even under a new coalition.
insights.quote: they promise to pursue broadly similar objectives but with greater competence
insights.locator: One of the enduring enigmas of Israeli politics

insights.claim: Most Israeli opposition parties remain reluctant even to articulate support for a two-state solution or an end to the occupation, and on Iran, Lebanon and Syria differ from Netanyahu mainly in style while maintaining reliance on military power.
insights.speaker: Chatham House
insights.so_what: Investors and policymakers should not price in a diplomatic pivot on the Palestinian issue or regional conflicts from a Netanyahu defeat.
insights.locator: This is particularly evident on the Palestinian issue

insights.claim: During the interim period after the Knesset's dissolution, Netanyahu remains head of a caretaker government retaining full executive authority, including the power to initiate military action.
insights.speaker: Chatham House
insights.so_what: A lame-duck leader with unchecked war powers is the key tail risk to watch in the weeks around and after the October vote.
insights.quote: Netanyahu will remain head of an interim government, retaining full executive authority, including the power to initiate military action.
insights.locator: Netanyahu will remain head of an interim government

insights.claim: An ageing, politically weakened and increasingly desperate Netanyahu could use military escalation on one or more fronts, potentially even against Trump's wishes, to manufacture a renewed sense of national emergency.
insights.speaker: Chatham House
insights.so_what: Iran, Lebanon, Gaza or the West Bank are named as potential escalation arenas; a spike in oil/defense risk premia around the election window is a plausible scenario to hedge.
insights.quote: an ageing, politically weakened and increasingly desperate prime minister could use escalation on one or more fronts to create a renewed sense of national emergency
insights.locator: There is concern that an ageing

insights.claim: The 2026 campaign is expected to be one of the most toxic and bitter in Israel's history, set against ongoing wars, fears of political violence and the real possibility the vote again fails to produce a decisive outcome.
insights.speaker: Chatham House
insights.so_what: Domestic political-violence risk and campaign toxicity add a further layer of instability to the near-term Israel outlook.
insights.locator: Yet the months ahead are likely to witness
